configure-and-initialize-a-repository.label: Initialize the Community Project Repository
configure-and-initialize-a-repository.summary: Turn a two-file folder into a repository on main and configure a repository-local identity.

sketch0.title: An Ordinary Folder Becomes a Repository

sketch1.title: Repository Metadata Stays Behind the Scenes

try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.title: Initialize the Community Project
try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.prompt: The terminal opens inside `community-project/`. The folder contains only `README.md` and `schedule.txt`, and it is not a Git repository yet. Initialize the current folder as a repository whose first branch is `main`. Do not edit or stage either file.
try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.hint: Create repository metadata in the current folder and name the first branch `main`.

help.concept: `git init -b main` creates the hidden `.git` metadata directory in the current folder and chooses `main` as the first branch. It does not edit, stage, or commit the project files.
help.hint_1: The terminal is already at the project root, so no `cd` command is needed.
help.hint_2: The result should be a repository with zero commits and two untracked files.

try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.starterCode:
# Community Project

Shared community activities and updates.
try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.instructions: The terminal opens inside `community-project/`. The folder contains only `README.md` and `schedule.txt`, and it is not a Git repository yet. Initialize the current folder as a repository whose first branch is `main`. Do not edit or stage either file.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf -- community-project
mkdir -p -- community-project
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s '# Community Project

Shared community activities and updates.
' > community-project/README.md
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s 'Community events

Saturday cleanup: 10:00 AM
' > community-project/schedule.txt

Saturday cleanup: 10:00 AM
' > community-project/schedule.txt

try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.title: Store the Local Author Name
try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.prompt: The repository now exists. Store the supplied author name `ZoeSkoul Learner` in this repository only. Do not change machine-wide Git settings.
try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.hint: Use repository-local configuration for the exact supplied name.

help.concept: `git config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"` stores the author name inside this repository. `--local` prevents the course workspace from changing Git settings outside this project.
help.hint_1: Keep quotation marks around the name because it contains a space.

try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.starterCode:
# Community Project

Shared community activities and updates.
try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.instructions: The repository now exists. Store the supplied author name `ZoeSkoul Learner` in this repository only. Do not change machine-wide Git settings.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf -- community-project
mkdir -p -- community-project
git -C community-project init -q -b main
find community-project -mindepth 1 -maxdepth 1 ! -name .git -exec rm -rf -- {} +
find community-project -mindepth 1 -maxdepth 1 ! -name .git -exec rm -rf -- {} +
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s '# Community Project

Shared community activities and updates.
' > community-project/README.md
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s 'Community events

Saturday cleanup: 10:00 AM
' > community-project/schedule.txt

find community-project -mindepth 1 -maxdepth 1 ! -name .git -exec rm -rf -- {} +
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s '# Community Project

Shared community activities and updates.
' > community-project/README.md
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s 'Community events

Saturday cleanup: 10:00 AM
' > community-project/schedule.txt

try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.title: Complete and Verify the Local Identity
try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.prompt: Store `learner@zoeskoul.local` as the repository-local author email. Then read the local configuration and confirm that both the supplied name and email are present.
try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.hint: Add the email in the same local scope, then inspect that scope.

help.concept: `git config --local user.email VALUE` stores the email for this repository. `git config --local --list` reads the local settings so you can verify both identity values before the first commit.
help.hint_2: The verification output should include both `user.name` and `user.email`.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf -- community-project

find community-project -mindepth 1 -maxdepth 1 ! -name .git -exec rm -rf -- {} +
find community-project -mindepth 1 -maxdepth 1 ! -name .git -exec rm -rf -- {} +
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s '# Community Project

mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s 'Community events

Saturday cleanup: 10:00 AM
' > community-project/schedule.txt

single-choice-1.title: Repository-Local Identity
single-choice-1.prompt: Why does this course use `git config --local` for the supplied author identity?
single-choice-1.hint: Choose the scope that changes only the current repository, not every repository on the computer.

help.concept: Turn an ordinary project folder into a Git repository on `main`, inspect the metadata Git created, and keep the author identity local to that repository.
help.hint_1: Remove options that act on a different repository layer than the one named in the question.
help.hint_2: Choose the answer that remains accurate even when other unrelated files exist.

options.a: It changes the identity only inside the current repository.
options.b: It deletes any existing project history.
options.c: It stages every file in the project.
options.d: It changes the contents of the project files.

single-choice-2.title: Repository Initialization
single-choice-2.prompt: What command is used to initialize a new Git repository?
single-choice-2.hint: Look for the Git command that creates repository metadata in an ordinary folder.

help.concept: Turn an ordinary project folder into a Git repository on `main`, inspect the metadata Git created, and keep the author identity local to that repository.
help.hint_1: Remove options that act on a different repository layer than the one named in the question.
help.hint_2: Choose the answer that remains accurate even when other unrelated files exist.

drag-reorder-1.title: Order the First Repository Setup
drag-reorder-1.prompt: Arrange the setup steps in an order that can actually work.
drag-reorder-1.hint: Start with the state that must exist before the next action can make sense.

help.concept: Turn an ordinary project folder into a Git repository on `main`, inspect the metadata Git created, and keep the author identity local to that repository.
help.hint_1: Track how the project moves from working files to a proposed snapshot and then to history.
help.hint_2: Read the ordered steps as one continuous repository story.

tokens.t1: Initialize the project folder as a repository

fill-blank-choice-1.title: Git Identity Configuration
fill-blank-choice-1.prompt: Complete the command to set the local Git user name: `git config --local user.name [blank1]`
fill-blank-choice-1.hint: Choose the term or symbol that matches the exact state described, not a nearby Git concept.

help.concept: Turn an ordinary project folder into a Git repository on `main`, inspect the metadata Git created, and keep the author identity local to that repository.
help.hint_1: Pay attention to whether the prompt describes an untracked path, a working-tree edit, staged work, or history.
help.hint_2: Read spaces, punctuation, and status columns as meaningful when the question shows Git output.

sketch0.title: Turn the Folder into a Repository
sketch0.bodyMarkdown: A Git repository is an ordinary project folder plus hidden history metadata. When a task asks you to run a command, type it in the terminal and press Enter. The command `git init -b main` creates that metadata in the current folder and names the first branch `main`. `git` runs Git, `init` initializes the repository, and `-b main` selects the initial branch. It does **not** edit, stage, or commit `README.md` or `schedule.txt`.

sketch1.title: Keep the Author Name Local
sketch1.bodyMarkdown:
Before Git can create a commit, it needs to know who the author is. The command `git config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"` stores the supplied name in this repository only.

The `--local` scope is important because it keeps this identity limited to the current project. It does not change Git settings for other repositories or for the learner’s entire computer.

Git also supports the `--global` scope, which applies an identity across all repositories for the current user. In this course, however, you will use `--local` so each practice repository keeps its own project-specific identity.

sketch2.title: Complete and Verify the Local Identity
sketch2.bodyMarkdown: `git config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"` stores the supplied email in this repository. `git config --local --list` then reads all local settings. Together, the name and email identify future commits; they are not a GitHub login.
